View this page

Demand verification¶

Once the resistance domain is computed, GenSec verifies load demands by measuring how close each demand point is to the domain boundary. The verification engine supports four utilization ratio types, staged combinations, and envelopes.

On this page

  • Utilization ratios

    • \(\eta_{\text{3D}}\) — 3D hull ray

    • \(\eta_{\text{2D}}\) — Mx-My plane ray

    • \(\eta_{\text{path}}\) — staged 3D ray

    • \(\eta_{\text{path,2D}}\) — staged 2D ray

  • Combinations

  • Envelopes

  • Convex hull

  • Output structure

  • Per-fiber post-processing

Utilization ratios¶

All four \(\eta\) types share the same geometric primitive — a ray from base \(\mathbf{B}\) through target \(\mathbf{T}\) intersecting a boundary at \(\mathbf{R}\):

\[\eta = \frac{|\mathbf{T} - \mathbf{B}|} {|\mathbf{R} - \mathbf{B}|}\]

Interpretation:

\(\eta < 1\) | Demand inside the domain — verified.

\(\eta = 1\) | Demand exactly on the boundary.

\(\eta > 1\) | Demand outside the domain — not verified.

\(\eta_{\text{3D}}\) — 3D hull ray¶

Ray from the origin \((0, 0, 0)\) through the demand \((N, M_x, M_y)\) to the 3D ConvexHull boundary.

Use case: all force components scale proportionally (overall load factor). Always fast — uses the 3D hull which is generated for any biaxial section.

Enabled by eta_3D: true (default).

\(\eta_{\text{2D}}\) — Mx-My plane ray¶

At the demand’s axial force \(N\), the Mx-My interaction contour is generated. A ray from \((0, 0)\) to \((M_x, M_y)\) is cast in this 2D plane.

This is equivalent to the \(\rho_M\) of VCASLU: it measures how close the moment vector is to the flexural boundary at the given axial force level.

Use case: fixed axial force, variable bending direction and magnitude (typical static design check).

Enabled by eta_2D: true (default false). Requires generating Mx-My contours on demand — slower than \(\eta_{\text{3D}}\).

\(\eta_{\text{path}}\) — staged 3D ray¶

For staged combinations, ray from the cumulative point of the previous stage \(\mathbf{S}_{k-1}\) through the current cumulative \(\mathbf{S}_k\) to the 3D hull boundary:

\[\eta_{\text{path},k} = \frac{|\mathbf{S}_k - \mathbf{S}_{k-1}|} {|\mathbf{R} - \mathbf{S}_{k-1}|}\]

Use case: measure the margin of a load increment relative to a known base state. The base is assumed certain; only the increment might exhaust capacity. Typical for seismic design (gravity base + seismic increment) and staged construction (prestress → gravity → variable).

Stage 0 has no predecessor — its \(\eta_{\text{path}}\) falls back to \(\eta_{\text{3D}}\).

Enabled by eta_path: true (default).

\(\eta_{\text{path,2D}}\) — staged 2D ray¶

Same concept as \(\eta_{\text{path}}\), but in the \(M_x\)-\(M_y\) plane at the target stage’s axial force.

Applicability condition: the axial force jump between stages must be small relative to the domain range:

\[\frac{|N_{S_k} - N_{S_{k-1}}|}{N_{Rd,\max} - N_{Rd,\min}} < \delta_N\]

where \(\delta_N\) is set by delta_N_tol (default 0.03, i.e. 3%). When the condition is not satisfied, \(\eta_{\text{path,2D}}\) is reported as null and a warning is emitted.

Rationale: if \(N\) changes significantly between stages, the base and target sit on different Mx-My contours, and a 2D ray in a single contour has no physical meaning.

Enabled by eta_path_2D: true (default false).

Combinations¶

A simple combination produces a single resultant from factored demands:

\[\mathbf{S} = \sum_i f_i \, \mathbf{d}_i\]

It is verified with \(\eta_{\text{3D}}\) and \(\eta_{\text{2D}}\) (if enabled).

A staged combination accumulates stages sequentially:

\[\mathbf{S}_k = \sum_{j=0}^{k} \Delta\mathbf{S}_j, \qquad \Delta\mathbf{S}_j = \sum_i f_{j,i} \, \mathbf{d}_{j,i}\]

Each stage reports its own \(\eta\) values. The eta_governing of the combination is the maximum across all stages and all enabled \(\eta\) types.

Envelopes¶

An envelope collects demands and/or combinations and reports the worst-case utilization:

\[\eta_{\text{envelope}} = \max_{\text{members}} \eta\]

Members can be:

  • References to named demands or combinations (ref).

  • Inline demands with direct N_kN, Mx_kNm, My_kNm.

  • Optionally scaled with a factor on the resultant.

Convex hull¶

The 3D domain boundary is represented as a scipy.spatial.ConvexHull in \((N, M_x, M_y)\) space. This is exact for convex domains (the typical case for RC sections at ULS under the parabola-rectangle law).

For 2D Mx-My contours, the domain at a given N is obtained by slicing the 3D hull and wrapping the result in a 2D ConvexHull. Contours are cached by the VerificationEngine to avoid redundant generation.

Output structure¶

The verification engine produces structured JSON results:

  • demand_summary.json: per-demand with all enabled \(\eta\).

  • combination_summary.json: per-combination with staged detail.

  • envelope_summary.json: per-envelope with governing member.

  • verification_summary.json: unified export of all three.

Each \(\eta\) field is present in the output only if the corresponding flag is enabled in the output block. The verified flag is true if all enabled \(\eta \le 1\).

Per-fiber post-processing¶

For any demand point, GenSec solves the inverse problem (find the strain plane that equilibrates the demand) and extracts the stress/strain state at every fiber and rebar:

  • FiberSolver.solve_equilibrium() — inverse solver.

  • FiberSolver.get_fiber_results() — stress/strain extraction.

Results are exported to CSV and plotted on the section geometry.
